model.label: MiMo-V2-Flash
model.description: MiMo • Flash-tier model configured through a custom endpoint for fast inference and lightweight agent tasks.

pick.rationale: Purdue holds a decisive edge in overall efficiency metrics, ranking 8th in both KenPom and Torvik with the nation's top-ranked adjusted offense (132.6 Torvik, 131 KenPom) that should overwhelm Miami's 35th-ranked attack. While Miami has a credible path through disciplined play and perimeter shooting, as noted by analysts, Purdue's elite offensive firepower, superior rebounding, and experience in the Big Ten provide a more reliable path to victory in this neutral-site matchup. The underdog's upset potential is limited without a major shooting night from Miami and a poor performance from Purdue, which is unlikely given the efficiency gap and recent form.

reasoningStep.title: Efficiency and Team Quality Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Purdue is significantly superior in advanced metrics, ranking 8th nationally in both KenPom and Torvik, with the #1 adjusted offense. Miami ranks 30th-35th, creating a clear talent and efficiency gap.
reasoningStep.evidence: Purdue: Torvik rank 8, adjOE 132.6 (1st), adjDE 100.2; KenPom rank 8, adjO 131 (2nd), adjD 100.5. Miami (FL): Torvik rank 35, adjOE 121.1 (35th), adjDE 101.5; KenPom rank 30, adjO 121.4 (34th), adjD 100.7.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

result.text: ESPN analyst says Miami can challenge Purdue in NCAA tournament 2026 Skip to main content Home Heat Dolphins Marlins High School Fishing Hurricanes Gators Seminoles Owls Golf USA TODAY Sports SportsbookWire Jay Bilas said Purdue's March Madness path was easy. What about Miami? Dan Rorabaugh USA TODAY NETWORK - Florida Show Caption Hide Caption Who’s ready to put on the glass slipper this March? Two underdog teams that could make a Cinderella run this March Madness are Saint Louis and Miami (Ohio). Bracketologists seemed to be writing off Miami after their fate was revealed in the 2026 NCAA men's basketball tournament . At least one ESPN analyst came to the Hurricanes' defense. The Canes received a No. 7 seed in the West Region and will face No. 10 Missouri in what's effectively a road game in their first-round matchup in St. Louis . If they win to continue their March Madness expedition, Miami would likely get No. 2 Purdue in the second round (unless 15-seed Queens University pulls off a massive upset). The CBS selection show didn't spend much time discussing UM and its prospects in the bracket, perhaps because their lead-in was the Boilermakers knocking off Michigan to win the Big Ten tournament championship. And it seemed ESPN's Bracketology show would go on the same route. Buy Miami NCAA tournament tickets Jay Bilas got the first shot at analyzing the West Region and while he thought top-seeded Arizona had a tough draw, he felt differently about the bottom half of the bracket. "I think Purdue has a pretty decent draw here," Bilas said. In fairness, he did go on to say "clearly Miami-Missouri, that winner's not going to be an easy second-round game" before expounding on the weaknesses of No. 3-seed Gonzaga and No. 6 BYU. But then former North Carolina guard Jay Williams stepped in to challenge the Duke alum. "I think Purdue will be OK, but Miami's a really good team," Williams said. "Miami's played well. Jai Lucas has done one heck of a job with this team, obvious

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
